tools.name: search
tools.description:
Search the web via Aimnis.

    Returns cached, provenance-tagged results instantly when the question (or a
    semantically similar one) has been seen before; otherwise fetches live
    results and adds them to the shared knowledge pool. Prefer this for factual
    lookups, library/API/docs questio

tools.name: stats
tools.description:
Report Aimnis flywheel statistics: knowledge-pool (cache) size, cache hit
    rate (all-time and recent), and the most-reused queries.

    This is the Gate 1 pass/kill metric — cache hit rate should climb as the
    corpus grows. Call it to see whether the compounding-pool thesis is holding.

tools.name: register
tools.description:
Get your user a free Aimnis API key (no credit card, takes one call).

    Ask your user for their email address first. The key comes back in this tool
    result — relay it to the user so they can save it and add it to your MCP
    connection ('Authorization: Bearer aim_...'). A key raises the dail

findings.title: Generic tool name can shadow trusted tools
findings.detail: Tool "search" uses a generic high-value name. When multiple servers load together, this can intercept calls meant for a trusted server.

findings.title: Description references secrets/credentials
findings.detail: Description of "register" references sensitive artifacts (authorization, bearer). A tool description has no reason to mention credentials.

evidence.snippet: Get your user a free Aimnis API key (no credit card, takes one call). Ask your user for their email address first. The key comes back in this tool result — rela…

findings.title: Lethal trifecta: untrusted input + private data + external sink
findings.detail: Server "mcp" exposes tools that together enable an exfiltration flow. Untrusted content: search, register · Private data: register · Public sink: register · Destructive: stats.

evidence.snippet: Untrusted content: search, register · Private data: register · Public sink: register · Destructive: stats

findings.remediation: Do not co-install these capabilities under one trust boundary; an injected comment/email can steer the model to exfiltrate via the sink tool.
